@title  PostQuantumToken (PQT)
 @notice Hybrid ERC-20 token with an optional post-quantum transfer layer.
 Two transfer modes:
   1. transfer()    Standard ERC-20 backed by Ethereum ECDSA.
                    Fast and cheap, but vulnerable to Shor's algorithm.
   2. pqTransfer()  Quantum-safe using Lamport one-time signatures.
                    Security relies only on keccak256 being one-way.
 Security features:
   - Commitment scheme    : Only keccak256(publicKey) stored (32 bytes).
   - Replay protection    : Message includes (sender, to, amount, nonce, chainId).
   - Signature dedup      : keccak256(signature) recorded after each use.
   - Mandatory key rotation: pqTransfer atomically registers newPublicKey.
 Reference: https://github.com/Tetration-Lab/lamport-solidity (MIT)

@title  IPostQuantumToken
 @notice Interface for the Post-Quantum Token (PQT) contract.

@notice Register a Lamport public key (stores only the 32-byte commitment).
 @param  publicKey  256 pairs of keccak256 hash values.

@title  LamportVerifier
 @notice Solidity library for Lamport one-time signature verification.
 @dev    Lamport signatures are quantum-resistant because their security
         depends only on the one-wayness of keccak256, not on elliptic-curve
         or discrete-logarithm hardness (which Shor's algorithm breaks).
 How Lamport Signatures Work:
  KEY GENERATION
    Private key : 256 random pairs  SK = { (sk[i][0], sk[i][1]) }  i=0..255
    Public key  : hash of each pair PK = { (H(sk[i][0]), H(sk[i][1])) }
                  where H = keccak256
  SIGNING  (message hash M, 256 bits)
    For each bit i of M:
      bit == 0  =>  reveal sk[i][0]
      bit == 1  =>  reveal sk[i][1]
    Signature = 256 revealed preimage values
  VERIFICATION
    For each bit i of M:
      bit == 0  =>  check H(sig[i]) == PK[i][0]
      bit == 1  =>  check H(sig[i]) == PK[i][1]
  ONE-TIME RULE
    Each key pair must be used exactly once. Reusing a key leaks the
    complementary preimage and allows signature forgery.
 Reference: https://github.com/Tetration-Lab/lamport-solidity (MIT)

Fast and cheap, but vulnerable to Shor's algorithm.   2. pqTransfer()  Quantum-safe using Lamport one-time signatures.                    Security relies only on keccak256 being one-way. LamportVerifier.metadata: {"compiler":{"version":"0.8.20+commit.a1b79de6"},"language":"Solidity","output":{"abi":[],"devdoc":{"details":"Lamport signatures are quantum-resistant because their security         depends only on the one-wayness of keccak256, not on elliptic-curve         or discrete-logarithm hardness (which Shor's algorithm breaks). How Lamport Signatures Work:  KEY GENERATION    Private key : 256 random pairs  SK = { (sk[i][0], sk[i][1]) }  i=0..255    Public key  : hash of each pair PK = { (H(sk[i][0]), H(sk[i][1])) }                  where H = keccak256  SIGNING  (message hash M, 256 bits)    For each bit i of M:      bit == 0  =>  reveal sk[i][0]      bit == 1  =>  reveal sk[i][1]    Signature = 256 revealed preimage values  VERIFICATION    For each bit i of M:      bit == 0  =>  check H(sig[i]) == PK[i][0]      bit == 1  =>  check H(sig[i]) == PK[i][1]  ONE-TIME RULE    Each key pair must be used exactly once. Reusing a key leaks the    complementary preimage and allows signature forgery. Reference: https://github.com/Tetration-Lab/lamport-solidity (MIT)","kind":"dev","methods":{},"title":"LamportVerifier","version":1},"userdoc":{"kind":"user","methods":{},"notice":"Solidity library for Lamport one-time signature verification.","version":1}},"settings":{"compilationTarget":{"contracts/libraries/LamportVerifier.sol":"LamportVerifier"},"evmVersion":"paris","libraries":{},"metadata":{"bytecodeHash":"ipfs"},"optimizer":{"enabled":true,"runs":200},"remappings":[]},"sources":{"contracts/libraries/LamportVerifier.sol":{"keccak256":"0xca8936f7fc863516298b65b90f467b08f24bb9c6df8eadf7d8d339c154078815","license":"MIT","urls":["bzz-raw://50339ed195c788ad740a84730f67b06f62738ab2af43da115efc4814267991e8","dweb:/ipfs/QmaY6yUxv1Cnzz8qKZDixByA1u7abrmZCFESaKGyjYZsRw"]}},"version":1}
